So my top sales manager was the laziest man I ever had in my life. Much lazier than me, but he was a very good sales manager. Did I answer your question? Yeah. It's family businesses have, I think, their own difficulties and stressors. My sons were extremely successful as salesmen for me. They were the top, up in the top 10 every month. So they did well. But when I died and went out of the picture and they had to run it for themselves, they didn't know how they couldn't do it. Thank you. Appreciate it. I'm not going, I wouldn't have done that that quickly, incidentally. I would have done it over a period of time and it might have been different. But the way I did it, it didn't work. It wasn't voluntarily. I didn't do it that well on purpose, but. Thank you. I had to. George, I was just wondering after you've acquired these clients, you made one large ticket sale to them. Do you have any sort of follow on program or any other? None. There was no back into this business at all. Dan talked to me about that years ago. My answer to that question was that I was doing so much in the front and busier than I knew what to do to start with. I was not a great office manager. I was a good office manager. But I was not great. I was a great promoter and motivator of people. I was wonderful. But I, office managing, I did adequately. And so I didn't go into other areas in the same business when I knew that if I had, well, simple thing, I could do that or I could put five more ads in for the same business. Why go into something else? That's where I was at that stage. Now, if I'd lived long enough, I'm sure I would have. Because the business was evolving and I know we would have, but we were still in the growth stage. He would have had to. It's the big change in the math of these things. So he was not alone, although he by far was maybe the biggest, if not one of the biggest. But there were a lot of opportunity marketers, as well as a lot of training companies that had little or no back end. But the cost per sale, there was a lot of profiting in the initial sale. So again, $100,000 of ad revenues driving $4 million worth of business. Those would not be your economics today. And so just like you could put that go by the edge show on today and you could build a business around it, but you'd have to treat the business very differently because we were buying half hours in major markets for $300 to run the show. And today you'd pay $6,000 for the same time block. So his cost per sale situation would be different. Everything else would work, but the CPS would be dramatically different. And so the businesses today, like even the $10 book guys, most of those guys had no back end. They didn't do anything with those customers. They sold them books and they rented the mailing list. That was like their back end. And some of them every once in a while through a seminar. I mean, that's it. There's not the sophisticated, you know, multi-step back end that you're familiar with today, but they really were in a position of heck why bother. We're not even running as many ads and sending out as many pieces as we could for this thing. And we're making a lot of money on it, right? Today that would not be the case. You know, this business to make that $10,000 sale today, the CPS might very well be $5,000, $6,000. And so the sophisticated back end on the business is now almost, you know, an economic essential. But with that said, any of these things that you looked at from the 60s and the 70s, almost all of them would work today if you attached the sophisticated back end to it. Thanks. Well, that was going to be my question about the back end. So maybe I can turn it into you want to tell the rest of the heart story? Just to irritate Dan? She wants to hear the rest of the heart story. The heart story. So we have time, you may pick up with... You're in Lexington. Where'd I stop? You're in Lexington. You're going in every month and you're waiting now for the heart. Well, when I went in, I had to put up $26,000 to go in. An hour ago it was 27,000. Really? It really was 26,000. I don't know, I got 27. Just for the record. Well, my wife says I have a tendency to do that. She says you always raise the dollar on everything. I was always the highest priced seller in my business. I started out in the cookware business, Cookware China Crystal Silverware. And I always sold for probably twice as much and I don't mean a little bit. I mean a lot a bit. And all of my competitors, I was always a top salesman in the company. And they were always saying I didn't have the right to sell it for as much because I was a crew of the people. And I said, well, I don't know, I sell it for what I have to sell in order to do my advertising budget in order to sell. So I've always spent a lot of money on advertising and put it in the price. I believe in it completely. And I think if you're scraping on your advertising, scrim on your office, I did not have a fancy office, had a very nice office, but I did not have a fancy office. And I didn't want it fancy because I wanted them to understand that they could run a business without a fancy office. Why do I want a fancy office? They're not going to have one. I was trying to explain to them, if you saw my literature, you'd know that I said that my wife and I started the business from our home in the basement of our home, which we did. And that's how it started. And it was just the two of us. And then I hired one man and two men and another woman, and then it took off from there. So I wanted them to understand that they could do the same thing.
